clips.viral_hook: So over the next few years, we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars in notional volume.
clips.transcript: So over the next few years, we believe that this market could explode up to five trillion dollars in notional volume. Now, right now, Cal, she is on an annualized run rate to do around one hundred and twenty one hundred and thirty billion dollars in notional volume for this year. So we're talking about a massive explosion of where this total market is headed. But when you put it into the perspective of the total derivatives market, which in 2024 was around seven hundred and fifteen trillion dollars in notional volume, you're only talking about 70 basis points, right? That five five trillion dollar figure over 715. So it's a drop in the bucket compared to where traditional derivatives sit today. And as you point out, I think this is an expansion of the addressable market that derivatives don't necessarily give you today in terms of the risk profile.
clips.description: ARK Invest predicts a massive growth in prediction markets, comparing the potential to the massive derivatives market.
clips.reasoning: High-conviction prediction with hard numbers and a clear data comparison to the total derivatives market.

clips.viral_hook: This movement is the beginnings, I think, of bringing active equity management back into style.
clips.transcript: this movement is the beginnings, I think, of bringing active equity management back into style because we're talking about events that should move stocks. And if we have more people looking for and at those events, they're going to be interested in investing in some of these opportunities and maybe taking a little bit out of that passive portfolio that is tied to the broader based indexes. So I'm pretty excited about this. This could be the beginning of a resurgence in active equity management. Yeah, absolutely. And I think when we've talked about it, prediction markets really represent the purest form of risk out there because as you point out, Cathy, you can go directly to the underlying discrete event that then moves the on an equity.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains why event-driven prediction markets will revive active equity management.
clips.reasoning: Counter-intuitive claim regarding passive vs. active management with a strong conviction.

clips.viral_hook: Really, you should be comparing debt to equity.
clips.transcript: what he does, trying to help people understand the debt and the deficit situation, is he says, you know, when you compare debt to GDP, what you're comparing is a balance sheet item to an income statement item, the equivalent of. Really, you should be comparing debt to equity. So equity is the black line, debt is the purple, and GDP is the red. And if we go to the next chart, you will see that while debt to GDP is above one here, the one ratio, you can see debt to equity is at 0.5. And actually it's very low by historical standards.
clips.shorts_title: The Real Way To View Debt
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains why debt-to-GDP is the wrong metric for the U.S. economy.
clips.reasoning: First-principles reframe of a commonly held economic belief using a chart.

clips.viral_hook: I actually think of these tools as almost the inverse of derivatives.
clips.transcript: I actually think of these tools as almost the inverse of derivatives, right? It's the underlying catalyst that will drive the performance of a stock up or down. And so whereas you may want to hedge out some of that risk using a put call spread or many other forms of hedging, now you have direct access to those underlying events, business KPIs, macroeconomic events where you don't need to take on the extra risk that is associated with the market, right? If you don't want to be exposed to what is happening in Iran, well, you don't need to take on that exposure through derivatives, which are going to inherently have some of that in it. You can go directly to the underlying event.
clips.description: How prediction markets offer a cleaner way to hedge specific risks compared to traditional derivatives.
clips.reasoning: Strong metaphor and first-principles explanation of a financial concept.

clips.viral_hook: Now we agree that it is going to go up, mostly because of what I just mentioned about foreign direct investment.
clips.transcript: Now we agree that it is going to go up, mostly because of what I just mentioned about foreign direct investment and our capital-friendly, business-friendly tax policies. Also, deregulation is, we think, going to increase the return on investment in the United States relative to that in the rest of the world. That is dollar positive. And we take this chart all the way back into the early 80s. Why? It is because we like to illustrate what happened during Reaganomics. And we think Trumponomics resembles greatly, actually, Reaganomics. And look what happened to the dollar back then. Now it was coming from the depths of despair in the late 70s. But it really went into overdrive to the point where all the treasury and monetary ministers around the world got together and said, this is becoming a deflationary risk to the rest of the world. Because a lot of debt is denominated, was and still is denominated in dollars. So the debt service was getting more and more expensive back then. We think we're not going to see a surge like we did in the early 80s. But we think the pressure is to the upside.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains why she expects the dollar to strengthen based on Reaganomics historical parallels.
clips.reasoning: Historical comparison and specific economic prediction regarding the dollar's value.

clips.viral_hook: All of those are highly deflationary trends.
clips.transcript: All of those are highly deflationary trends. And could the yield curve be picking up on that? We think it might. Now, here's productivity, which is a part of that story. The technology, the innovation platforms that I just mentioned involve 15 different technologies. They're converging. And we think that productivity growth is going to soar in the next few years. So here we ask the question, will non-farm productivity increase above 3% in any quarter? This is on a year-over-year basis, in any quarter in 26. And you can see there's a 42% probability. Well, we think the probability actually is much higher than that.
clips.description: Why AI, robotics, and blockchain are deflationary forces according to Cathie Wood.
clips.reasoning: First-principles reframe of inflation and technology with a clear conviction.

clips.viral_hook: We think that productivity growth is going to soar in the next few years.
clips.transcript: We think that productivity growth is going to soar in the next few years. So here we ask the question, will non-farm productivity increase above 3% in any quarter? This is on a year-over-year basis, in any quarter in 26. And you can see there's a 42% probability. Well, we think the probability actually is much higher than that. And it's actually for a statistical reason. If you look at productivity today, you can see we're at roughly 2.5%. And you can also see, again, if you look across this chart, this is in the higher half of the history of this chart. So pretty strong. And as we have said in the know many times, we've been in a rolling recession and this is what has happened. That's so interesting. Housing has been down and out. Manufacturing has been down and out. Small business down and out. Consumers have been in a funk, as measured by sentiment. And productivity is at 2.5%. So we believe that these technologies are already having an impact on productivity.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains why the U.S. economy is poised for a productivity explosion.
clips.reasoning: Strong prediction with a timeframe and specific data points.

clips.viral_hook: The market is pretty sure that producer price inflation is going to surpass consumer price inflation.
clips.transcript: the market is pretty sure that producer price inflation is going to surpass consumer price inflation on a year-over-year basis for three consecutive months this year. Now, that's very interesting. Now, what is this saying? Well, you can see it started out closer to 25 or 20 percent probability, moved to 30 as the initial stages of the war in Iran took off. And then everyone thought it was going to be over quickly, settled back, and you can see what has happened since. This is more extended than many observers predicted. And so that's where there's certainty so far as you can see on the next chart.
clips.description: Why the disconnect between producer and consumer prices signals a squeeze on corporate margins.
clips.reasoning: Data reveal showing a disconnect between producer and consumer prices.

clips.viral_hook: We're bringing in younger people with one foot through the AI door.
clips.transcript: We're bringing in younger people with one foot through the AI door and very eager to introduce us to new productivity enhancing ideas. Maybe that's what's going on a little bit more broadly here I don't know. Here you can see another stress point saving rate down it dropped to four percent in today's report as income was very weak but spending again mostly prices was lifted and so a low saving rate is it typically takes place when consumers have no choice and I think that's where we are right now or when there's an incredible amount of optimism out there and more and more people are willing to live on the edge a bit more. We think it's more the former than the latter.
clips.description: Cathie Wood on why they are hiring younger, AI-literate talent to boost productivity.
clips.reasoning: Personal conviction and insight into changing workplace dynamics due to AI.

clips.viral_hook: Velocity actually has been in a downtrend since the late 90s.
clips.transcript: velocity actually has been in a downtrend since the late 90s. And that is when baby boomers started to retire. Those were the very early baby boomers. But also, it was around the time that China entered the WTO. And there was a lot of concern in the United States. Our manufacturing sector effectively was exported to China. And so, there were concerns. And I think those two variables caused the secular decline. Well, baby boomers are still retiring. And again, when they retire, they're not as free spending. And we are now seeing, however, the reversal of the shipment of our manufacturing sector over to China. And I think our tax package is one reason for that. So, maybe we're seeing a secular turn in velocity. If not, it will probably flatline here. And, you know, because baby boomers are retiring, but the manufacturing is coming back and other kinds of investment coming back. Maybe we flatline here.
clips.description: Cathie Wood explains the secular decline of money velocity and why it might be reversing.
clips.review_reason: Economic theory might be dense for a general audience, but it's a solid reframe.
clips.reasoning: First-principles reframe of a complex economic concept.

clips.viral_hook: The total government spending is at about seven trillion dollars.
clips.transcript: the total government spending is at about seven trillion dollars. And so one of that is defense. Two of that is health care. And one of the downward pressures on the budget is health care. We've heard about Medicare actually cutting prices dramatically in terms of some of the most heavily used drugs out there. We're also seeing Doge continue to cut back on federal employment. And yet we do have this war. So the odds have gone down, certainly have in the short term. But if we if we get past this period into fiscal 27, we think we'll have another shot at getting below 5 percent and heading actually very quickly to 3 percent. And the reason for that is we see GDP growing so much more quickly than many economists expect.
clips.description: Breaking down the $7 trillion U.S. government budget and why the deficit could drop.
clips.review_reason: Specific focus on government spending, which may be divisive.
clips.reasoning: Hard numbers on government spending and a prediction for the deficit path.

clips.viral_hook: We're seeing the S&P to gold and we've been watching this carefully.
clips.review_reason: Longer explanation of multiple indicators; may need tight trimming.
clips.transcript: We're seeing the S&P to gold and we've been watching this carefully. Remember it was it was a leading indicator for some very bad times in the 70s and there are some strategists out there who think we're going back into those bad times. We do not but this is uncomfortable in that it has tipped down. This is the offset to some extent and until very recently it's S&P to crude oil and that uptrend is still in place even with the really near more than 50% increase in oil prices. Here's Bitcoin to gold. Bitcoin is starting to outperform gold again. Gold peaked January 28th, the day that Warsh was announced as the next Fed chairman if Congress approves him. And he is slightly hawkish. So Bitcoin has been moving up relative to gold. That's interesting. And then just the last two charts, two or three charts here. This one is still a puzzle. Why is that green line, metals to gold, still going down? Is it something really wrong in China? I don't know if it's just China but it is puzzling especially now that the gold price has gone down from nearly, I think it was almost, was it $5,600 down to $4,400. We haven't seen a clear turn in this variable which is quite interesting. It used to correlate very well with the 10-year treasury yield which you see in purple there. They parted ways in 22 when the Fed started tightening and I would say when central banks started selling gold, I mean buying gold and selling dollars. And again the yen carry trade unwinding starting in the summer of 24. So all of those should be in there now and my guess is that the 10-year treasury yield is probably going to fall because we've had a lot of major forces pushing it up, not the least of which has been the recent flare-up in commodity prices. If we're right and supply, the supply side of the economy is coming to the rescue, we're probably going to be surprised at interest rates coming down as opposed to going up. And a final chart here, final two charts here, credit default swaps on banks, you know, private credit, is this infecting, is it going to be a systemic issue? This chart would suggest no, it's not backing up into the banking system. And this is the high yield chart, so junk bonds relative to treasuries. They're not seeing anything on the horizon bothersome. In fact, they're near all-time lows. So these charts, these last two charts, would say nothing to worry about.
clips.viral_hook: We're seeing the S&P to gold and we've been watching this carefully.
clips.description: Cathie Wood reviews key market indicators to see if we're heading for a crisis.
clips.reasoning: Summary of market indicators providing a 'cliffhanger' feel about market health.
